claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 10 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 10 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin your Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.**

**How old is the child?**

Please let me know which category applies:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you're claiming for multiple children, we can go through each one - just let me know their ages and names so I can assess them individually.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim Child Benefit for my two children - Alex, who's 5 years old, and Blake, who's 10. So they're both under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! It's my pleasure to help you with this. Great news that both Alex (5) and Blake (10) are under 16 - that's a good start for eligibility!

Let me navigate to the next step for children under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Given their ages (5 and 10), I'd expect they're not in paid employment - but I just need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not working at all - they're just kids! Alex is only 5 and Blake is 10, so they're both in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense - they're both young children in school. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This means they shouldn't be personally claiming benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit themselves. At ages 5 and 10, this would be very unusual, but I need to confirm - are both children free from receiving such benefits in their own name?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - neither of them receives any benefits in their own name. They're just children, so no, they don't get ESA or Universal Credit or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's exactly what I'd expect for children their age. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are Alex and Blake in any apprenticeships?**

At ages 5 and 10, I'm confident they won't be, but just to confirm - neither child is enrolled in an apprenticeship programme, correct? They're both just attending regular school?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're definitely not in any apprenticeships - they're far too young for that! Alex is in primary school and Blake is still in primary school too. Just regular school for both of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly right - far too young for apprenticeships! Both in primary school is perfectly clear. Let me move on to the next part of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You only need to meet **ONE** of these to qualify:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You're a UK resident living here
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3+ continuous months)
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can support yourself financially (without claiming certain benefits)
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are a student with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as a family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You're living abroad as a Crown servant

Which of these applies to you? For most people, option 1 (normally living in the UK) is the most common.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm a UK resident living here. So option 1 applies to me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's the most straightforward path. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You qualify as responsible if **any** of the following apply:

1. **Living with the children** - Alex and Blake live with you
2. **Contributing to their upkeep** - You pay at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **Paying maintenance** - You make maintenance payments under a court order or agreement

Do any of these apply to you for both children?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both Alex and Blake live with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful, that's clear. Let me proceed to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

This could be another parent, a grandparent, or anyone else. There are three possibilities:

1. **No one else is claiming** - You're the only person claiming Child Benefit for these children
2. **Someone else claims but you've agreed** - Another person has been claiming, but you've both agreed that you should now be the one to claim
3. **There's a dispute** - Another person is claiming and you haven't agreed on who should receive the benefit

Which situation applies to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No one else is claiming Child Benefit for them - I'm the only person claiming for Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that keeps things straightforward. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Where are Alex and Blake currently living?**

I need to check if any special circumstances apply. Are the children:

1. **In hospital** - Currently staying in hospital
2. **In care** - Living in local authority care
3. **Fostered** - In a fostering arrangement
4. **Living normally at home** - In a normal home living situation with you

Which applies to both Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're both living normally at home with me - no special circumstances like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's the most straightforward situation. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question: Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex and Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, you're covering the children's living costs yourself, and the local council isn't contributing to their accommodation or upkeep. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - the local council isn't paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance. I'm covering all their living costs myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That completes the assessment. Let me navigate to the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great news! 🎉

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## ✅ Assessment Result: ELIGIBLE

**Congratulations!** Based on the information you've provided, you are **eligible for Child Benefit** for both **Alex** and **Blake**.

### Summary for each child:

| Child | Age | Eligible? | Key Reasons |
|-------|-----|-----------|-------------|
| **Alex** | 5 | ✅ Yes | Under 16, lives with you, no other claimant, no council support |
| **Blake** | 10 | ✅ Yes | Under 16, lives with you, no other claimant, no council support |

---

### 📝 Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** for Alex and Blake

### 💷 Payment Information:
- Payments are made **every 4 weeks** (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### ⚠️ Important Note:
If you or your partner earns over **£60,000 per year**, you may be subject to the **High Income Child Benefit Charge**. You can still claim, but some or all of the benefit may need to be repaid through your tax return.

---

You can apply here: [https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex (age 5) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) Under 16 years old - automatic age qualification, (2) Not in paid work, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) Parent is a UK resident, (6) Parent is responsible for the child (lives with them), (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Living normally at home, and (9) Local council is not paying towards accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 10) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) Under 16 years old - automatic age qualification, (2) Not in paid work, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) Parent is a UK resident, (6) Parent is responsible for the child (lives with them), (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, (8) Living normally at home, and (9) Local council is not paying towards accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children. They are a UK resident who lives with both children (Alex, 5, and Blake, 10), is solely responsible for them, and covers all their living costs without local council support. Both children are under 16, in regular primary school, not working, and not receiving any benefits in their own right. All eligibility criteria have been met for both children.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?

Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 5) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) Under 16 years old - automatic age qualification, (2) Not in paid work, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) Parent is a UK resident, (6) Parent is responsible for the child (lives with them), (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Living normally at home, and (9) Local council is not paying towards accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 10) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) Under 16 years old - automatic age qualification, (2) Not in paid work, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) Parent is a UK resident, (6) Parent is responsible for the child (lives with them), (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, (8) Living normally at home, and (9) Local council is not paying towards accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children. They are a UK resident who lives with both children (Alex, 5, and Blake, 10), is solely responsible for them, and covers all their living costs without local council support. Both children are under 16, in regular primary school, not working, and not receiving any benefits in their own right. All eligibility criteria have been met for both children.
